countries.name: United States
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is expected and forms a major part of service workers' income. Not tipping is considered very rude.

countries.name: Canada
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping culture is similar to the US. 15-20% is standard for most services.

countries.name: Mexico
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping (propina) is customary and appreciated. Service workers rely on tips to supplement low wages.

countries.name: United Kingdom
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not as expected as in North America. Check if a service charge is already added to your bill.

rules.note: 10-15%, check for service charge on bill
£5-10 for half day, £10-20 for full day
Not expected, round up if you wish
Not expected at pubs, optional at cocktail bars

countries.name: France
countries.etiquette_note: Service charge (service compris) is included by law. Small extra tips are a kind gesture but never expected.

rules.note: Service included; leave small change or 5% for great service
Small tip of a few euros

countries.name: Germany
countries.etiquette_note: Service charge is included in prices. Rounding up or adding 5-10% is customary for good service. Say the total you want to pay rather than leaving money on the table.

rules.note: Round up or 5-10%; tell server the total amount
Round up to nearest euro or 10%

countries.name: Spain
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included in the bill. Leaving small change is a nice gesture but not expected.

rules.note: Leave loose change or 5-10% at nice restaurants
Not expected, round up if desired

countries.name: Italy
countries.etiquette_note: A coperto (cover charge) is common and separate from tips. Service is often included. A small extra tip for excellent service is appreciated.

rules.note: Check for servizio incluso; leave €1-5 for great service

countries.name: Portugal
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not mandatory but appreciated. Leaving 5-10% or rounding up is a common practice.

countries.name: Netherlands
countries.etiquette_note: Service charge is included by law. Rounding up or leaving 5-10% for excellent service is appreciated.

rules.note: Service included; round up or 5-10% for great service

countries.name: Belgium
countries.etiquette_note: Service and VAT are included in prices. Tipping is not expected but rounding up is polite.

rules.note: Service included; round up for great service

countries.name: Austria
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included but rounding up is very common. Say the total amount you wish to pay when settling the bill.

rules.note: 5-10%, tell server the total you want to pay

countries.name: Switzerland
countries.etiquette_note: Service charge is included by law. Wages are high so tips are less important, but rounding up is appreciated.

rules.note: Service included; round up or 5% for good service

countries.name: Ireland
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not obligatory. 10% is generous. Check if service charge is added to your bill.

rules.note: 10% is generous; check for service charge

countries.name: Luxembourg
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included. Tips are not expected but rounding up is a nice gesture.

rules.note: Service included; round up or leave change

countries.name: Malta
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated. Some restaurants add a service charge.

countries.name: Monaco
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included. Additional tips are welcome given Monaco's luxury dining scene.

rules.note: Service included; 5-15% extra at upscale venues

countries.name: Liechtenstein
countries.etiquette_note: Similar to Switzerland. Service is included and tipping is not expected but rounding up is polite.

rules.note: Round up or leave small change

countries.name: Sweden
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included. Tipping is not expected but rounding up or leaving 5-10% is appreciated for good service.

countries.name: Norway
countries.etiquette_note: Service is included and wages are high. Tipping is not expected but appreciated for excellent service.

rules.note: Round up or 5-10% at nice restaurants

countries.name: Denmark
countries.etiquette_note: Service charge and VAT are included. Tipping is not customary but rounding up is a nice gesture.

rules.note: Service included; round up or leave change

countries.name: Finland
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not part of Finnish culture. Service charge is included. Tips are never expected.

rules.note: Not expected; round up if you wish

countries.name: Iceland
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not part of Icelandic culture. Service is always included. Extra tips may confuse staff.

countries.name: Estonia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is becoming more common, especially in Tallinn. 10% is generous.

countries.name: Latvia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not obligatory. 10% is considered generous in Riga.

countries.name: Lithuania
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 5-10% is standard in restaurants.

countries.name: Japan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is NOT customary and can be considered rude or confusing. Excellent service is the standard and expected without tips.

rules.note: Do NOT tip. It may be seen as insulting
Not expected; a small gift is more appropriate

countries.name: South Korea
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not part of Korean culture. Service charges are included at upscale restaurants and hotels.

countries.name: China
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional in China and can sometimes cause embarrassment. Upscale international hotels may accept tips.

countries.name: Taiwan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not customary. A 10% service charge is added at upscale restaurants.

rules.note: 10% service charge added at upscale venues; no extra tip needed

countries.name: Hong Kong
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is standard at restaurants. Additional tipping is not expected but appreciated.

rules.note: 10% service charge usually added; leave small change extra

countries.name: Macau
countries.etiquette_note: Similar to Hong Kong. 10% service charge is common at restaurants and casinos.

rules.note: 10% service charge added; small extra appreciated

countries.name: Mongolia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but becoming more common in Ulaanbaatar tourist areas.

countries.name: Poland
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard and appreciated. Tell the server the total amount you want to pay.

rules.note: 10% standard; tell server total you want to pay

countries.name: Czech Republic
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is customary in Prague. Outside Prague, rounding up is common. Never leave tip on the table—tell the server.

rules.note: 10%; tell server, don't leave on table

countries.name: Hungary
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Always check if service charge is included on the bill first.

rules.note: 10%; check for service charge on bill

countries.name: Romania
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is common and appreciated, especially in Bucharest.

countries.name: Bulgaria
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard in restaurants. Smaller tips are appreciated for other services.

countries.name: Slovakia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is customary. Round up and tell the server the amount.

countries.name: Slovenia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not obligatory. 5-10% for good service.

countries.name: Croatia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is common, especially in tourist areas along the coast.

countries.name: Serbia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard and appreciated in Belgrade restaurants.

countries.name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated in restaurants.

countries.name: Montenegro
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is common in tourist areas along the coast.

countries.name: North Macedonia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated at restaurants.

countries.name: Albania
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory. 5-10% is generous.

countries.name: Moldova
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated.

countries.name: Ukraine
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard and very appreciated in restaurants.

countries.name: Greece
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is customary. Leave it on the table in cash even if paying by card.

countries.name: Cyprus
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Some restaurants add a service charge.

countries.name: Turkey
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is customary and appreciated. Round up for smaller services.

countries.name: Israel
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 15-20% is expected at restaurants, similar to the US. Service workers rely on tips.

countries.name: Georgia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is becoming more common in Tbilisi. 10% is generous.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated in tourist areas. Leave change or round up. High-end restaurants may add 10% service charge.

rules.note: Leave change at street food; 10% at upscale restaurants

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated in tourist areas. International hotels and restaurants may add a service charge.

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not mandatory but appreciated, especially in Bali tourist areas. Some restaurants add service charge.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is common at restaurants. Additional tips are welcome and much appreciated.

rules.note: 10%; check for service charge on bill

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is added at most restaurants. Additional tipping is not expected.

rules.note: 10% service charge usually included; no extra needed

countries.name: Singapore
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge plus 9% GST is standard (often shown as '++' on menus). No additional tip expected.

rules.note: 10% service charge included; no tip needed

countries.name: Myanmar
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but becoming appreciated in tourist areas.

rules.note: Round up or 5-10% at nicer restaurants

countries.name: Cambodia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated and meaningful given low wages. USD is widely accepted.

countries.name: Laos
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not expected but appreciated, especially from tourists.

rules.note: Round up or 5-10% at nicer places

countries.name: Brunei
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not customary. A 10% service charge is added at most hotels and restaurants.

rules.note: Service charge included; no tip needed

countries.name: Timor-Leste
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not expected but greatly appreciated given low incomes.

countries.name: India
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is expected at restaurants and hotels. Many add a service charge. Small tips are appreciated for daily services.

rules.note: 10%; check for service charge on bill

countries.name: Pakistan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% is generous at restaurants.

countries.name: Bangladesh
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated and meaningful. 5-10% is generous.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is common at hotels and restaurants. Additional tips appreciated.

countries.name: Nepal
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% is generous. Tips for trekking guides are especially important.

countries.name: Maldives
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is added at resorts. Additional tips in USD are welcome for outstanding service.

rules.note: 10% service charge added; extra $5-10 for great service

countries.name: Bhutan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated for guides and drivers.

countries.name: Afghanistan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 5-10% at restaurants.

countries.name: United Arab Emirates
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is expected. 15-20% at restaurants even when a service charge is added. Cash tips preferred.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 15-20% is expected and appreciated. Many restaurants add a 15% service charge.

countries.name: Qatar
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is expected at restaurants. Service charge may be included.

countries.name: Kuwait
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard. A service charge may be added but doesn't always go to staff.

countries.name: Bahrain
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard.

countries.name: Oman
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Service staff are generally low-paid.

countries.name: Jordan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is expected and appreciated. 10% is standard.

countries.name: Lebanon
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard. USD widely accepted and often preferred.

countries.name: Egypt
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping (baksheesh) is deeply embedded in culture and expected for nearly all services. Always carry small bills.

rules.note: 10-15%; even if service charge added, tip waiter directly

countries.name: Russia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard in Moscow and St. Petersburg restaurants. Less common outside major cities.

countries.name: Kazakhstan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is becoming more common in Almaty and Astana. 10% is generous.

countries.name: Uzbekistan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated, especially from tourists.

countries.name: Brazil
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge (gorjeta) is usually added to the bill. Check before tipping extra.

rules.note: 10% usually on bill; extra for great service

countries.name: Argentina
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Leave cash even if paying by card.

countries.name: Colombia
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% voluntary service charge (propina voluntaria) is common. You'll be asked if you accept it.

countries.name: Peru
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated. Upscale restaurants may add a service charge.

rules.note: Not expected; negotiate fare in advance

countries.name: Chile
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% tip is standard. Most restaurants show a suggested 10% on the bill.

countries.name: Ecuador
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is added by law but doesn't always go to staff. Extra tips are welcome.

rules.note: 10% already added; extra $1-2 for great service

countries.name: Venezuela
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is common. Additional tips in USD greatly appreciated.

countries.name: Bolivia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not expected but appreciated. 5-10% is generous.

countries.name: Paraguay
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not mandatory but 10% is appreciated.

countries.name: Uruguay
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard at restaurants.

countries.name: Guyana
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated.

countries.name: Suriname
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory.

countries.name: Costa Rica
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge is added by law. Extra tips are welcome but not expected.

countries.name: Panama
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% tip is standard. Some restaurants add a service charge.

countries.name: Guatemala
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard and appreciated.

countries.name: Cuba
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping in USD or EUR is greatly appreciated and important for locals given low state wages.

countries.name: Jamaica
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is expected, similar to the US. All-inclusive resorts may have no-tipping policies.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
countries.etiquette_note: A 10% service charge and 18% tax are added. Extra cash tips are appreciated by staff.

countries.name: Bahamas
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 15-20% is expected, similar to the US. USD accepted everywhere.

countries.name: Barbados
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard. Check if service charge is added.

countries.name: Trinidad and Tobago
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard at restaurants.

countries.name: Puerto Rico
countries.etiquette_note: US territory - tipping culture is the same as mainland US. 15-20% expected.

countries.name: Honduras
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard.

countries.name: Nicaragua
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated.

countries.name: El Salvador
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard.

countries.name: Belize
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard. USD widely accepted.

countries.name: Haiti
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated and meaningful.

countries.name: South Africa
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard and expected. Service workers rely heavily on tips.

countries.name: Kenya
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Tips for safari guides are especially important.

countries.name: Tanzania
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. Safari and Kilimanjaro guides rely on tips.

countries.name: Ethiopia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% is generous.

countries.name: Nigeria
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated at restaurants.

countries.name: Ghana
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated at restaurants.

countries.name: Morocco
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is expected for most services. 10% at restaurants. Small tips appreciated everywhere.

countries.name: Tunisia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard and appreciated.

countries.name: Algeria
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated but not expected. 5-10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Australia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not expected due to high minimum wages. A small tip for exceptional service is appreciated but never required.

rules.note: Not expected; 5-10% for exceptional fine dining
Not expected; you can say 'keep the change'

countries.name: New Zealand
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not part of NZ culture. Fair wages mean tips are never expected. A small gesture for great service is fine.

rules.note: Not expected; round up or 5-10% for great service

countries.name: Fiji
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional in Fijian culture and can even be offensive. Resort staff may accept tips from tourists.

rules.note: Not expected; small tip at tourist restaurants OK

countries.name: Uganda
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% at restaurants. Safari guides expect tips.

countries.name: Rwanda
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. Gorilla trekking guides rely on tips.

countries.name: Mauritius
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard at restaurants. Resort staff appreciate tips.

countries.name: Seychelles
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated at restaurants and for tour guides.

countries.name: Botswana
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. Safari guides and camp staff expect tips.

countries.name: Namibia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. Safari and activity guides depend on tips.

countries.name: Senegal
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 5-10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Ivory Coast
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 5-10% is appreciated.

countries.name: Cameroon
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 5-10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Madagascar
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated and very meaningful due to low wages.

countries.name: Zambia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated at restaurants. Safari guides expect tips.

countries.name: Zimbabwe
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard. USD is the main currency. Tips are very meaningful.

countries.name: Iran
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard at restaurants. Service charge may be added.

countries.name: Iraq
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Kyrgyzstan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is becoming more common in Bishkek. 10% is generous.

countries.name: Tajikistan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated from tourists.

countries.name: Turkmenistan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated.

countries.name: Yemen
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard at restaurants.

countries.name: Palestine
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is appreciated at restaurants.

countries.name: Libya
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Mozambique
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated.

countries.name: Angola
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Malawi
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated and meaningful. 10% at restaurants.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard.

countries.name: Antigua and Barbuda
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is expected.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10-15% is standard.

countries.name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard.

countries.name: Grenada
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is appreciated. Check for service charge.

countries.name: Papua New Guinea
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not traditional but appreciated at tourist establishments.

countries.name: Samoa

countries.name: Tonga
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not customary.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not customary.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated. 5-10%.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is appreciated.

countries.name: Armenia
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is becoming standard, especially in Yerevan.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard in Baku restaurants.

countries.name: Jersey
countries.etiquette_note: Similar to UK. 10% at restaurants.

countries.etiquette_note: Similar to UK. 10% at restaurants.

countries.name: Isle of Man
countries.etiquette_note: Similar to UK. 10% at restaurants.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 10% is standard in Minsk.

countries.etiquette_note: Similar to UK. 10% at restaurants.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 15-20% is expected. Similar to US/Dutch norms.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping 15-20% is expected.

countries.etiquette_note: US territory. Tipping 15-20% expected, same as mainland.

countries.etiquette_note: French territory. Service included. Small extra is polite.

countries.etiquette_note: Tipping is not part of Polynesian culture. Service is included.

countries.name: Guam
countries.etiquette_note: US territory. Tipping same as mainland US.

countries.name: Saint Martin
